headlines.title: Anthropic urges industry coordination to allow for a ‘pause' in AI development if risks grow
headlines.description: Anthropic is proposing that the world’s top artificial intelligence companies come up with a coordinated way to pause development of advanced AI systems, warning the technology is improving so quickly there’s a risk humans would lose control. The company behind the Claude chatbot said in a blog post Thursday that as cutting-edge AI gets increasingly faster at carrying out tasks, “it would be good for the world to have the option to slow or temporarily pause” its development. Anthropic said its internal research institute plans to explore the issue in collaboration with others and “take actions” to help build the systems for a credible slowdown or pause, without being more specific. Anthropic rival OpenAI argued for a different approach in a report published Wednesday, saying that “democratic governments — not private companies acting alone — must ultimately determine the rules, safeguards, and accountability mechanisms.” “Our view is that decisions about the pace of AI innovation should not be left to any one lab, company, or special interest group,” it said. AI models are getting faster, with rapid increases in how quickly they can carry out software tasks like coding on their own, Anthropic said in its post. Based on current trends and given enough computing power, an AI system could be able to design and develop its own successor, in what is known as “recursive self-improvement.” Self-building AI would be a major technological milestone that would bring benefits in science, healthcare and other areas, Anthropic said, but it “also might increase the risks of humans losing control over AI systems.” Some tech industry figures have long warned of such a scenario. Anthropic’s post comes after a different warning this week from a team of researchers at the University of Toronto who showed how AI tools could be used to create a new kind of AI “worm” that adapts its hacking strategy as it spreads from device to device and takes over a vast computing network. “I think it’s really important that people understand that it’s not just the biggest, most powerful language models that pose the security concerns,” lead researcher Nicolas Papernot said in an interview. The authors of the Anthropic post, company co-founder Jack Clark and Marina Favaro, head of its research institute, said the pause would be used to enable “societal structures and alignment research” to keep up with AI advances. Alignment is industry shorthand for making sure the technology matches human values and intentions. The proposed coordination would let advanced AI labs verify that global rivals have actually stopped or slowed their work, “and that a bad actor could not use the auspices of a coordinated slowdown to jump ahead in secret.” The company said a coordinated global mechanism is needed because without it a slowdown in AI development could let the “least cautious” players catch up and add to pressure on companies and governments as they make tough choices about AI safety. Anthropic’s post comes as the company and ChatGPT-maker OpenAI race to sell shares on the stock market, in an IPO that could value Anthropic at nearly a trillion dollars. Papernot notified Canadian cybersecurity authorities prior to releasing his report, which shows how researchers developed the worm in a laboratory by using an “open-source” AI tool that is easy for software developers to cheaply access and modify. “In the past, cyber attackers would focus on targets that are very high value,” he said. “Banking systems, hospitals, electricity grids, water treatment systems, schools.” Papernot agreed that there should be more collaboration between companies, government agencies and academic researchers to develop countermeasures as AI-powered hacking tools supercharge the search for computer vulnerabilities. “That old laptop you have in your basement that you don’t check on regularly doesn’t seem like a very high-value target, but It can be used as a launch pad to attack these higher-value targets,” he said. “Anything connected to the internet is now at risk because of how low the cost has become to mount these cyberattacks.”
headlines.source: Kelvin Chan | The Associated Press and Matt O'brien | The Associated Press

headlines.title: Police were not at Fairfield graduation when deadly shooting happened
headlines.description: Officials in Fairfield revealed Thursday that there were no police officers at the high school campus where a deadly shooting happened the day before after a high school graduation ceremony. The shooting happened Wednesday at Fairfield High School, where the Sem Yeto Continuation High School had just finished holding its graduation. Officials have now confirmed that one 18-year-old wearing graduation robes was killed in the shooting, and three other people (an 11-year-old and two people in their 20s) were injured but expected to survive. During a conversation with reporters Thursday at Fairfield High School, Fairfield Mayor Catherine Moy said, “I do know there were no officers here when the shooting occurred.” Moy noted that Fairfield High School does have a school resource officer, but that resource officer was not on campus at the time. Moy explained that she had been in touch with the Police Chief an hour and a half earlier. Fairfield 18 hours ago Fairfield Jun 3 By Thursday evening, the Fairfield Police Department posted to social media that it is “aware that it has been suggested that the Fairfield Suisun Unified School District did not request our presence at the Sem Yeto graduation ceremony,” and clarified “this is not an accurate characterization of what occurred.” The post continued that the police department held an internal review along with the school district and found “an attempt to make a request was submitted for the Sem Yeto graduation.” “The request was not made through the established channels, and the department was not aware of the request.” Moy replied to post, saying she asked the city manager if there were any police officers at the graduation. She said he “told me no. He explained that the school district had not requested police coverage. He further told me that the city doesn’t usually cover the smaller school graduations, unless requested.” She said he confirmed the Armijo and Fairfield High graduations would be fully staffed, but she added that, based on the information the police department provided, “it’s unclear what occurred” and “clearly there was a failure of the established process. It’s important that we see where that failure occurred, so it doesn’t happen again.” Moy told NBC Bay Area she calls for officers at all high school graduations and added “I know the police chief and city manager agree”.

headlines.title: Attorney chimes in after San Francisco family sees nearly 90% rent increase
headlines.description: The San Francisco family whose rent is increasing nearly 90% is getting some relief after a city supervisor reached out to offer some help. On Wednesday, NBC Bay Area shared the story of the Waldman family, who received a notice on their door, telling them the rent at their two-bedroom unit in the Richmond District would be going up from about $3,000 to $7,000 a month in September. The notice came after new owners bought the building. Zachary and Ashley’s story have generated a lot of reaction online, with people calling the increase outrageous. San Francisco Supervisor Connie Chan, who represents the area, said her office contacted the Waldmans to connect them with legal resources. Meanwhile, this isn’t the first time attorney Rahman Popal has heard of cases like theirs. “I’ve seen rent increases over 100%, actually I filed several lawsuits of rent increases of 150% or more,” he said. Even though many cities have strict rules on how much rent increases can be, he says there are exceptions to city and state rent control laws. Zachary and Ashley were under the impression they were living in a rent-controlled apartment, but their building is listed as a condo, which opens up a loophole. Popal says there are a number of steps tenants can take. “First and foremost you want to check your lease, to see whether or not there’s an exemption from AB 1482 thats included in the lease. Without the exemption from 1482 in the lease, then your landlord is required to comply with the rent caps at the state level,” he said. Popal recommends those in the same situation to review their case with an attorney. “The attorney is going to be probably better situated to look at all of these different factors to determine if you have a viable case for a defense,” Popal said. NBC Bay Area’s Business and Tech Reporter Scott Budman says the median rent has increased in the city, but $7,000 is high. “That’s high for what we normally talk about but consider this: The median rent in San Francisco for one bedroom is $4,000, and that’s the first time the city has hit that,” he said. The story prompted reactions from many in the Bay Area, who said it is expensive to live in the area. Budman says there are two factors for that: The tech and low inventory. “AI is bringing extremely wealthy tech population, AI is bringing high salary,” he said. “A combination of a lot of money in the hands of techies who can cash in their stock options and very low inventory leaving not all that much for the lower end.”

headlines.title: Volunteers begin setting up San Francisco's Pink Triangle for Pride Month
headlines.description: San Francisco’s iconic Pink Triangle is being installed on Twin Peaks ahead of Pride Month celebrations. Volunteers are scheduled to begin outlining the massive symbol Friday at the top of Twin Peaks. Additional volunteers will return Saturday to fill in the triangle. The Pink Triangle was originally used by the Nazi regime to identify and dehumanize LGBTQ people. Over time, activists reclaimed the symbol and transformed it into one representing solidarity, resilience and pride. Organizers say there are still a few volunteer spots available for those interested in helping with this year’s installation. More information and volunteer sign-ups are available at ThePinkTriangle.com.

headlines.title: Are GLP-1 compounds safe? Not from this SF company and WA supplier, whistleblowers say
headlines.description: GLP-1 medications are the hottest way to lose weight, but are all versions of the drug being marketed to you safe? A San Francisco-based weight loss startup, Mochi Health, is currently under a firestorm of scrutiny after working with pharmaceutical supplier Aqueita Pharmacy, which is in Kirkland, Washington. Partnering with the NBC station in Seattle, Washington, NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit obtained public records showing whistleblowers went to state inspectors last year with a long list of patient safety complaints against Aequita with which Mochi’s CEO Myra Ahmad was directly involved, they said. Myra Ahmad, Mochi Health’s CEO. About 800 miles north of Mochi Health’s headquarters at 333 Bush Street in San Francisco’s Financial District, Mochi worked with Aequita to create GLP-1 compounded medication – at one point shipping 20,000 prescriptions to patients a week across the U.S., inspectors said in their findings. GLP-1 compounds are mixed by pharmacies to mimic brand name weight loss drugs. They’re not FDA approved and the federal agency says they can be risky. Whistleblower reports, state findings As Ahmad took to social media talking about Mochi’s growth and cheaper GLP-1 options for patients, Aequita pharmacy staff met with state inspectors in 2025 sharing ring videos from the facility. The videos appear to show Aequita’s Head of Operations bring in unlicensed temporary workers to process prescriptions. The workers were picked up at home improvement store parking lots, according to six pharmacy employees who spoke with King 5, the NBC station in Seattle. Ring video shows unlicensed temporary workers entering Aequita to work on prescriptions. “They would come over to our main facility…and actually handle the pharmaceuticals themselves,” former Aequita lab manager Paul Meyers said. “They didn’t have any state licensing.” Regulators substantiated this complaint. They also found Aequita allowed untrained pharmacy assistants to compound the GLP-1 prescriptions and even directed the untrained pharmacy workers to compound the drugs without proper supervision. “The pharmacy is allowing pharmacy assistants to compound in the clean room, despite not having completed all the necessary compounding training…” Washington Department of Health regulators According to the state’s report, whistleblowers told investigators Ahmad made a lot of the risky cost-cutting decisions at Aequita. For instance, one employee said Ahmad asked her to find the unlicensed temporary workers to specifically help “handle sterile compounded drugs and assemble kits for shipping and delivery to patients.” Expired drugs, glass particles in vials, and Otter Pops Whistleblowers also told investigators, according to the state’s report, that Ahmad and Aequita’s Head of Operations decided to switch to cheap medicine vials that ended up breaking and shattering. In its determination, the state said witnesses reported seeing glass particles in some of the compounded product. It’s unclear if it made it to patients. Also in their findings, inspectors said they discovered expired GLP-1 prescriptions that were going to be mailed out to patients. At least three had been expired for nearly nine months. Then, when the pharmacy ran out of ice packs to ship with the prescriptions because the medication needs to be kept cold during transit, inspectors said staffers alleged Ahmad suggested using frozen Otter Pop popsicles instead. Patients complained online that some of the popsicles leaked, spilling all over their medication, which the state mentioned in its report. Instead of ice packs, some Mochi patients said they received their prescriptions with Otter Pops as cooling mechanisms. Video: Regulators visit GLP-1 supplier unannounced In March 2025, Washington Department of Health inspectors visited one of Aequita’s warehouses unannounced. They were responding to whistleblower reports, which included allegations the pharmacy moved GLP-1 drug ingredients across the street to hide them from inspection. Video from that visit obtained through a public records request by NBC Bay Area and the NBC station in Seattle shows a warehouse manager denying knowledge of any attempts to evade inspectors. Washington Department of Health inspectors visited Aequita unannounced in March 2025. “As far as I know there’s nothing from the, the pharmacy…This is just storage for the packaging and stuff like that,” the manager told regulators during the inspection. “Well, that’s not necessarily true. I can tell you that…that’s not packaging,” an inspector responded opening a refrigerator next to the warehouse manager. In the refrigerator were bags of peptide base powders that, according to the state, Aequita used in its GLP-1 compounds. Business records show, months before, Myra Ahmad formed a wholesaler company, USA Distribution LLC, linked to that warehouse. Inspectors said USA Distribution wasn’t licensed to be a wholesaler yet when inspectors found the powders. As a result of the inspection and its investigation into the whistleblower reports, the Washington Department of Health issued Aequita a Notice of Immediate Jeopardy. The regulator said the pharmacy’s “deficient practices … have placed the health and safety of individuals at risk of serious injury, serious harm, serious impairment or death.” Washington state officials said Aequita Pharmacy closed this past April. “[Aequita’s] deficient practices or conditions have placed the health and safety of individuals at risk of serious injury, serious impairment, or death.” Washington Department of Health regulators Mochi Health’s CEO avoids questions, no response from Aequita NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit has tried to speaking with someone at Aequita and Myra Ahmad about these allegations since April, but we haven’t received a response. At the Livelong Women’s Health Summit in San Francisco last March, NBC Bay Area tried speaking with Ahmad in person. She didn’t answer any questions and walked away. Myra Ahmad declined to answer reporter questions at a health conference in March. In a booming industry that’s marketing the hottest way to lose weight, Mochi is still offering GLP-1 compounded medication to patients, according to its website, working with other pharmacies nationwide. Candice Nguyen is the senior investigative reporter on this story. If you have a question or comment, email her at candice.nguyen@nbcuni.com.

headlines.title: VTA ready to transport thousands of fans during World Cup
headlines.description: With the 2026 World Cup set to kick off in less than a week, Valley Transit Authority already is warning fans that public transportation will be the best way to get to the stadium. On Friday, international consulates teamed up with the South Bay transit agency to help ensure their citizens can get to the San Francisco Bay Area stadium with ease. VTA officials said the agency is preparing for increased demand and expects to move tens of thousands of international visitors during the tournament. The transit agency is encouraging fans to plan their trips ahead of time and use public transportation to travel to matches. May 13 2026 World Cup May 19 2026 World Cup May 8 World Cup brings heightened security risks. What's the Bay Area preparing for?

headlines.title: Mail thieves get ahold of election ballots in the South Bay
headlines.description: Mail thieves got ahold of election ballots in the South Bay early Monday morning, prompting a warning from the U.S. Postal Inspection Service. Vote counting continues at the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters’ office in San Jose, but NBC Bay Area learned that more than two dozen ballots were brought in by postal inspectors after thieves got to them. According to the Postal Inspection Service, the unopened ballots were delivered directly to the Registrar of Voters after being recovered from bins of stolen mail. “It was about 29 ballots,” said Matt Moreles of the Santa Clara County Registrar of Voters. “They were all sealed inside their return envelopes. So we were able to signature check them and verified they were valid and then we did count them.” Postal inspectors said the ballots were found outside a Grocery Outlet store in Morgan Hill, where police discovered multiple bins of stolen mail, including the sealed ballots. “Mail thieves are looking for financial instruments that they can get out of the mail,” inspector Matthew Norfleet said. “And sometimes they pick up some election mail. There was no reason to think the election-related mail was targeted. If anything it was left behind.” Norfleet said it remains unclear where in Santa Clara County the mail was stolen. Investigators believe it could have been taken from a blue collection box or a community mailbox often found at apartment complexes. The Postal Inspection Service is urging residents across the county to check with banks, businesses and loved ones for any mail that should have been delivered between Sunday night and Monday morning. For voters who want to make sure their ballots are counted, Moreles encouraged them to use the county’s ballot tracking tools. “You can log onto our website, sccvote.org, and we have a look-up tool there,” Moreles said. “We also have a service that we go through, through the state, called Where’s-My-Ballot where you can sign up for text alerts or email alerts, so that as soon as we check in your ballot, you get notified as well.” The Postal Inspection Service is continuing to search for the thieves. Investigators recommend that residents avoid leaving outgoing mail in blue collection boxes or other drop-off locations overnight to reduce the risk of theft.

headlines.title: Exclusive: Cain Velasquez sits down for first TV interview since release from prison
headlines.description: Former UFC champion Cain Velasquez sat down with NBC Bay Area for his first television interview since serving time in prison for attempted murder. Velasquez, who landed behind bars after targeting a man accused of molesting one of his young relatives, was released from Soledad State Prison in February. Today, the 43-year-old convicted felon is rebuilding his life and mentoring fighters. He hopes his story can teach younger people as he continues to learn about himself. At American Kickboxing Academy (AKA) in San Jose, Velasquez has found a new purpose. He said he is both a student and teacher within the gym’s walls. “It’s always both,” he said. “I’ve always been a teacher even though I didn’t necessarily think I was or I didn’t want to. But now I’m taking more on that road of teaching others, but I’m still looking to learn.” Back in 2022, Velasquez targeted a man he says molested one of his relatives. The chase and shooting was caught on video. He was sentenced to five years in prison on charges that included attempted murder and assault. He eventually was released for good behavior and time served. He remains on parole, and a civil case is still pending. “In the last four years of getting things taken away…I’ve always stayed really just true to myself and how I want to continue with life and that’s living from a place of love and giving back,” he said. South Bay Feb 16 Crime and Courts Mar 24, 2025 He admitted to mistakes in the past. Moving forward, he wants to be of service and give back in community. “It all just starts with forgiving yourself,” he said. “Hopefully somebody can see my story and hopefully that can help them as well. They can learn from my mistakes as well.” Velasquez trains and teaches at AKA, a mixed martial arts gym and mecca for fighters around the world. Before the hitting and sparring, there is the breathing. Velasquez calls it “ascension breath.” He’s been practicing it for years. “The breathing helps you get rid of or work some traumas, things that you haven’t let go of but also the day-to-day, let go of things in the moment so you can just focus on what you’re doing here, and that’s the specific form of combat,” he said. Velasquez shares these lessons not just in the ring but in prison when he was at Soledad. “That’s why I started doing it in there,” he said. “I felt like these men here needed it the most and could benefit from this the most.” Velasquez will hold his very first “Ascension Breath” event on Saturday at the San Jose Marriott. Space is limited. Tickets are available at rollnrumble.com.
headlines.source: Raj Mathai, Christine Ni and Jeremy Carroll

headlines.title: Redwood City company develops pill to treat pancreatic cancer, and trial results are promising
headlines.description: A Redwood City company developed a pill to treat one of the deadliest types of cancers, and is showing promising results. The experimental drug is called daraxonrasib and it was developed by Revolution Medicines. The pill is for patients with pancreatic cancer and is taken once daily. In a recent trial, patients with pancreatic cancer saw their survival rates double from about six and a half months with chemotherapy, to more than 13 months with the new pill. The results were so promising the pill is now being fast-tracked by the FDA. Dr. Mark Goldsmith, the CEO and Chairman of Revolution Medicines, explained how the drug works in the video above.

headlines.title: U.S. soccer star Naomi Girma talks her village, inspiring the next generation
headlines.description: San Jose’s Naomi Girma is among the elite women’s soccer stars overseas and on Team USA. The Stanford graduate now lives in London, playing for Chelsea. In 2025, the club signed Girma for $1.1 million, the highest transfer fee ever paid for a female soccer player. Her worldwide recognition starts with her village in the Bay Area. NBC Bay Area’s Janelle Wang talked to Girma about her Ethiopian heritage, growing up in the Bay Area and her message to younger athletes. Watch their conversation in the video player above. 2024 Paris Olympics Jul 1, 2024 soccer Jul 11, 2023 My New Favorite Futbolista: Naomi Girma

headlines.title: AI takes center stage at Snowflake Summit in San Francisco
headlines.description: More than 20,000 people from all over the world flocked to the Snowflake Summit in San Francisco Thursday to talk about artificial intelligence and their own futures in the industry. Most of the companies on the Moscone Center floor insisted that while they’re building AI bots, they’re also hiring humans. “Humans are the ones building the applications, that are going to use them, so of course there’s no point of agents if there are no humans,” Kloudgen Vice President Neha Mittal said. NBC Bay Area’s Scott Budman has more in the video report above. Economy Jun 4 Artificial intelligence Jun 2 Trump signs executive order that invites vetting of top AI models for national security risks

headlines.title: Oakland boosts security ahead of First Fridays street festival
headlines.description: Oakland officials on Thursday unveiled an enhanced, multi-agency public safety initiative designed to curb late-night violence ahead of the city’s monthly “First Fridays” street festival. The move follows several post-event fights and violent incidents in the downtown and uptown corridors, raising safety concerns for festival organizers, visitors and local businesses. “Violence is not an option in Oakland,” Mayor Barbara Lee said. “The safety enhancements that we are rolling out target the late-night hours.” The Oakland Police Department will increase staffing from Friday night into Saturday morning. The local deployment will be bolstered by support from the California Highway Patrol and the Alameda County Sheriff’s Office. Law enforcement officials said the collaborative effort will prioritize traffic violations, illegal parking and driving under the influence. Police will also deploy drones to monitor large gatherings and assist in identifying suspects if criminal activity occurs. “This is the first time in my time addressing these issues that all of our departments are coming together as one to serve our residents and our merchants and our visitors,” OPD Interim Chief James Beere said. “It’s a game changer in my view.” In addition to traditional law enforcement, the city’s Department of Violence Prevention will be on the ground to monitor unauthorized youth gatherings, known as “teen takeovers,” alongside the illegal sale of alcohol and drugs. The security strategy comes after members of the local business community demanded intervention. Tony Leong, general manager of the Fox Theater, was among several business leaders who recently petitioned city officials for specific enforcement plans. “These takeovers, they don’t come out of nowhere — they’re planned,” Leong said. “We know that they’re coming. We know when it’s happening. Hopefully we have a resolution for it, and over the course of time, what we’re asking for, too, is consistency.” Venessa McGhee, director of Oakland First Fridays, said the recent spike in nearby violence has already crippled sponsorships and the event’s future. “I do think it is a little late to the game — a lot of the damage, at least to our event, has already been done,” McGhee said. “In all transparency, we are scrapping together just to make tomorrow happen.” City leaders say the pilot program will continue to evolve with financial support from business partners. Assistant City Administrator Michelle Phillips noted that “there is a collaborative effort so that resource burden, financially, isn’t all on the city.” City council members also emphasized that security concerns in the entertainment district extend beyond a single monthly festival. “I also want to be clear that it is not just First Fridays,” Councilmember Carroll Fife said. “We need public safety infrastructure every single night of the week.” Officials said this weekend’s festival will serve as the initial test for the coordinated safety approach.
